skill.name: routeros-mndp
skill.description: MNDP (MikroTik Neighbor Discovery Protocol) wire format, behavior, and RouterOS /ip/neighbor integration. Use when: implementing MNDP discovery, parsing MNDP packets, working with /ip/neighbor, understanding WinBox device discovery, debugging why a router doesn't appear in neighbor lists, or when the user mentions MNDP, neighbor discovery, WinBox discovery, or /ip/neighbor.
skill.summary: Implement MikroTik device discovery using the MNDP protocol
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected 44 external_commands and 5 network patterns. After evaluation, all external_commands are documentation of code examples showing proper protocol implementation patterns (dgram socket, setsockopt). The backtick patterns in SKILL.md are embedded code samples in markdown, not runtime execution. Network patterns include hardcoded IPs (255.255.255.255, 192.168.88.1) which are legitimate broadcast addresses and example values. No malicious intent found; skill is safe for publication.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance - Network Information Disclosure
low_findings.description: The skill documents MNDP as a protocol that reveals device identity, version, board model, IP addresses, MAC addresses, and uptime. This is expected behavior for a discovery protocol, not a vulnerability in this skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: This documents legitimate protocol behavior. MNDP information disclosure is inherent to the protocol specification, not a flaw in this skill's implementation.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer flagged 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' at 44 locations in SKILL.md. These are markdown code blocks documenting protocol implementation examples (TypeScript, C, shell). The backticks are documentation delimiters for code samples, not runtime command execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Confirmed false positive: all flagged locations contain markdown code blocks with TypeScript, C, or shell code examples showing proper dgram socket creation and setsockopt usage. No actual backtick command execution exists in this documentation file.

content.user_title: Discover MikroTik devices using MNDP protocol
content.value_statement: Implementing MikroTik device discovery requires understanding the MNDP wire format and RouterOS neighbor APIs. This skill provides the protocol specification, TLV parsing examples, and REST API patterns to build reliable neighbor discovery into your Claude or Claude Code workflows.
actual_capabilities: Parse MNDP wire format with TLV encoding for device identity, version, board, MAC, and IP extraction
Implement UDP broadcast listener for 255.255.255.255:5678 to discover RouterOS devices
Query RouterOS /ip/neighbor via CLI and REST API to retrieve cached discovery results
Handle multi-interface RouterOS devices that send separate MNDP announcements per interface
Understand timing expectations from 1-3 seconds (LAN) to 10-30 seconds (high-latency networks)
Distinguish between WinBox discovery (MNDP) and network scanning for correct tool selection
limitations: This skill provides documentation and examples; actual implementation requires writing code in your preferred language
MNDP discovery is limited to the local broadcast domain; devices across routers will not appear
This skill does not provide the RouterOS API connection handling; see the routeros-fundamentals skill for SSH/REST authentication

use_cases.title: Build a network inventory script
use_cases.description: Create a script that discovers all MikroTik devices on your network segment and outputs their identity, board model, and software version. Useful for auditing device fleets without accessing each router individually.

use_cases.title: Debug missing router in neighbor list
use_cases.description: When a RouterOS device does not appear in WinBox or your inventory, use the MNDP timing and packet format knowledge to verify discovery is working. Check interface configuration and timing expectations.

use_cases.title: Integrate device discovery into Claude Code workflows
use_cases.description: Add MikroTik device discovery to your Claude Code automation pipelines. Query /ip/neighbor via REST to get current router neighbor status as part of network health checks.

prompt_templates.title: Parse MNDP packet bytes
prompt_templates.prompt:
I have an MNDP response packet as raw bytes. Parse the TLV records and extract identity, version, board, MAC address, and IP address.

Packet hex: [provide hex data]

Show the parsed fields with their TLV type numbers.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you have a captured MNDP packet from a packet capture

prompt_templates.title: Query RouterOS neighbor list
prompt_templates.prompt: Connect to a MikroTik router at [router-ip] and query the /ip/neighbor table via REST API. Return all discovered devices with their interface, address, mac-address, identity, platform, and version fields.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need to retrieve cached neighbor data from a router

prompt_templates.title: Explain MNDP TLV type
prompt_templates.prompt: What is TLV type 0x0011 in the MNDP protocol? Explain the value format, byte order, and how to parse it with code examples in TypeScript.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need to understand a specific field in the protocol

prompt_templates.title: Implement MNDP listener
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a Node.js TypeScript function that creates a UDP socket listening on port 5678 with SO_REUSEPORT and SO_BROADCAST enabled. Include self-echo filtering and parse incoming MNDP response packets into structured fields.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need to build a custom MNDP discovery client

output_examples.input: I need to find all MikroTik routers on my network. How do I discover them?
output_examples.output:
To discover MikroTik devices, you can:

1. **Check RouterOS /ip/neighbor**: Query the router itself via CLI or REST API to see cached discovery results from all connected interfaces.

2. **Send an MNDP refresh packet**: Broadcast a 9-byte UDP datagram to 255.255.255.255:5678 (type=0x0000, sequence=0x0000). All RouterOS devices will respond with TLV-encoded announcements.

3. **Expected response time**: 1-3 seconds on LAN, up to 10-30 seconds on high-latency networks. Send multiple refresh packets at 5-second intervals for reliability.

Devices respond from their own IP on the receiving interface, with their identity (hostname), board model, software version, and MAC address.

output_examples.input: What information does MNDP reveal about a router?
output_examples.output:
MNDP responses contain these TLV fields:

- **Type 1**: MAC address (6 bytes, big-endian)
- **Type 5**: Identity/hostname (UTF-8 string)
- **Type 7**: Software version (e.g., '7.18 (stable)')
- **Type 8**: Platform (usually 'MikroTik')
- **Type 10/13**: Board model (e.g., 'RB4011iGS+5HacQ2HnD')
- **Type 11**: Uptime in seconds since boot
- **Type 16**: Interface name that sent the response
- **Type 17**: IPv4 address of the sending interface

Note: RouterOS devices send separate MNDP announcements per interface, so the same router may appear multiple times with different MACs and IPs.

output_examples.input: A router is not appearing in my neighbor list. How do I debug this?
output_examples.output:
Debug steps for missing router in neighbor list:

1. **Verify the router sends MNDP**: Check `/ip/neighbor/discovery-settings/print` on the router and confirm MNDP is enabled.

2. **Check network connectivity**: The router must be on the same broadcast domain. MNDP does not cross routers.

3. **Review timing expectations**: Local LAN responds in 1-3s, but high-latency or tunnel networks may take 10-30s.

4. **Check interface configuration**: Some interfaces may be excluded via interface lists. Run `/interface/list/print` to review.

5. **Verify no firewall blocking**: UDP port 5678 must be allowed on both ends.

6. **Consider multi-interface behavior**: A router with N interfaces sends N separate announcements - group by identity to avoid duplicates.

best_practices: Send multiple MNDP refresh packets at 5-second intervals during the listen window to handle packet loss, WiFi power-save delays, and tunnel relay latency
Group discovered devices by identity (hostname) when displaying results, since multi-interface routers send separate announcements per interface
Query the RouterOS /ip/neighbor table directly via REST API when you need cached results rather than implementing raw socket discovery from scratch
anti_patterns: Do not assume a device is offline if it does not appear in a short scan window; increase timeout before concluding a device is unreachable
Do not implement raw packet parsing without checking TLV bounds; malformed packets can cause buffer overruns
Do not display the same router multiple times; group by identity and use MAC address to disambiguate when the identity is the factory default ('MikroTik')

faq.question: What is the difference between MNDP and network scanning?
faq.answer: MNDP is a discovery protocol that RouterOS devices participate in by default. When you send a refresh packet to 255.255.255.255:5678, all MikroTik devices on the broadcast domain respond. Network scanning (ping sweep, port scan) finds any IP host regardless of type. Use MNDP when you specifically need MikroTik devices.

faq.question: Why does the same router appear multiple times in results?
faq.answer: A RouterOS device with multiple interfaces sends a separate MNDP announcement from each interface. Each announcement has a different MAC, IP, and interface name but the same identity (hostname). Group by identity and use MAC to disambiguate when the identity is the factory default.

faq.question: How long should I wait for MNDP responses?
faq.answer: Expected response times: Local LAN (wired) 1-3 seconds, WiFi or congested networks 3-10 seconds, ZeroTier or tunnel overlays 5-20 seconds, satellite or high-latency links 10-30 seconds. Send refresh packets every 5 seconds during your listen window.

faq.question: Can MNDP discover devices across different subnets?
faq.answer: No. MNDP uses Layer 2 broadcast and does not cross routers. Devices can only be discovered on the same broadcast domain. For multi-subnet discovery, you need to query /ip/neighbor on a router in each subnet.

faq.question: What information does MNDP reveal about my router?
faq.answer: MNDP exposes: hostname (identity), software version, board model, MAC address per interface, IP address per interface, uptime, and interface names. Treat MNDP like an open SNMP community - disable it on untrusted interfaces.

faq.question: How do I access MNDP results from RouterOS via REST API?
faq.answer: Query `/rest/ip/neighbor` with basic authentication. The response is a JSON array with columns: interface, address, mac-address, identity, platform, version, board. Example: curl -u admin:password http://<router-ip>/rest/ip/neighbor
